item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just really enjoyed the 2481 reference. I read it, doubted it, headcanoned it anyway, and then it was confirmed here. Thank you for always doing such great crosslinking, it's an absolute delight.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This would be fun for the How the World Ends/Competitive Eschatology canon. Hate having a bit of an existential crisis

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Skoll and Hati are why the sun and moon move through the sky; they're being chased by wolves. Their father is Fenrir, a wolf who is destined to eat Odin Allfather and was cruelly bound in a chain made of things that don't exist. HIS father is Loki Liesmith, scapegoat of the gods.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perhaps low to zero gravity, to be on the safe side (in case of emergencies where Dr. Sigurd is stuck in space).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 3609's ability to move about in space is OK, but I think that Dr. Sigurd does not have to train in zero-gravity. 1/6 gravity should be enough for him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice article, but I have to say that lunar surface is not zero-gravity, but 1/6 gravity.

Indeed. That being said, I figured that 3609 can move about in space as well. Perhaps I would include 'low gravity' for the sake of clarity.

Thus the word "Jötunn" should be "Jǫtnar", which is plural form of Jǫtunn.

Amended.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And one more, Æsir is plural form of Ass. Thus the word "Jötunn" should be "Jǫtnar", which is plural form of Jǫtunn.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice article, but I have to say that lunar surface is not zero-gravity, but 1/6 gravity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah. It's fine in casual conversation, but I feel like its something the Foundation would be particular about, since deduce would imply it was something they didn't have in a database before.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"It was determined to be" would probably be more accurate here.

In reflection, I would have used 'identified'. But 'deduced' does have specific connotations that I really should think through before I use it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Minor grammatical nitpick

"Said vocalisations were recorded, and it was later deduced to be Old West Norse."

I don't believe 'deduce' would be the correct term here, unless the Foundation ran it through every other language in their database first and someone said "Well by process of elimination, it HAS to be Old West Norse."

"It was determined to be" would probably be more accurate here.

Otherwise, I enjoyed the article. I'll give it until my coffee kicks in for a re-read, then I'll see if it still lives with me enough to upvote it.

item.title: Re: Thanks, Attribution, and Other Matters
item.content:encoded:
It's more like Hati was shrunk down to be more on par with a normal wolf. If it were in the setting before the beams of light, Hati would be monstrously large.

Now, the Norse gods conform to the logic of the current world – and wolfs cannot be gigantic.

But frankly, I came up with that part since it was deemed unrealistic to contain a giant wolf underneath the Moon.

item.title: Re: Thanks, Attribution, and Other Matters
item.content:encoded: Awww I kind of liked the idea that the Norse gods were all just really small.

item.title: Re: Thanks, Attribution, and Other Matters
item.content:encoded:
Allow me to express thanks to… mlister

Well I feel accomplished.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I will just be lazy and redefine him as half-Faroese and half-Icelandic, with his name being a compromise.

But I really chose 'Sigurd' since it was easy to spell on the standard English keyboard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The connection to Moon Wizard is a little on the goofy side, I'm sorry.

That connection was meant to establish that it cannot smell on the Moon (but only be able to do so inside the base and within the Moon Wizard's domain), which hints at the shift from hostility to not-hostility.

Inside the moon base, it would realise that the Foundation smell human instead of being Aesir or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You might be thinking of a giant wolf skip AbsentmindedNihilist made and then deleted, which was underneath ice and upset that dogs let themselves be domesticated by humans. It wasn't Norse though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No such Icelandic name as 'Sigurd', if you were indeed going for an Icelandic researcher. Sigurður is the modern Icelandic equivalent.

I think Sigurd might be a name in Faroese. But if he's Faroese, I don't think they would spell 'Ólafsson' that way. Probably 'Ólavsson'.

(Don't feel bad for getting it wrong — this is the third or fourth time I've corrected someone for writing an article with an improper Icelandic name!)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I swear I've seen this once before, and the wolf was buried under ice or something. I just remember "Norse myth wolf talking to researcher" and also something about being surprised about how people look.

The connection to Moon Wizard is a little on the goofy side, I'm sorry.

item.title: Re: Poor guy
item.content:encoded: Nobody induces existential crises in mythological entities better than Foundation personnel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Understand.

item.title: Poor guy
item.content:encoded:
Dr. Sigurd: What about you? What will you be doing when Fenrir kills Odin? Or when Víðarr kills Fenrir?

SCP-3609: I, I am not sure.

Dr. Sigurd: It is as you have said, your purpose ends when you consume Máni and Fenrir breaks loose. Do think about it. If we're having this engaging conversation, you're certainly more than a means to an end.

[SCP-3609 ceases vocalisation and prowls around its containment cell. Dr. Sigurd attempted to call for SCP-3609 attention, to no avail.]

You…you broke him…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Usually, I prefer to wait until I am certain that the article will stick before putting the nickname. That way, it saves me the trouble of having to retype [ACCESS DENIED] if I end up self-deleting it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You forgot the title in series page.
alright already change.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ugh. I kind of just glazed over a huge chunk of the article because I don't know shit from fuck w/r/t Norse mythos. I feel like I'm missing a lot here? No vote, this could definitely stand to be friendlier to someone who doesn't know Norse mythology, but maybe I'm weird for not getting this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Additionally, it is sapient and able to speak Old West Norse.

This sticks out really badly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
then continue feeding it moon rocks if they knew it didn't sustenance to survive?

Valid point raised. I updated it such that the moon rocks are to keep the SCP compliant, after having probed its eating habits and figuring out that moon rocks are highly likely to keep it not taking out its aggression on the moon base.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a wolf that wants to eat the moon. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay I totally did not realize what the ten beams of light are and now I feel foolish XD Either way, I'm glad that's a thing.

Great job! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wasn't there a skip with King Arthur on the moon too?

E: I was thinking of SCP-2824.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Moon Cat will save the day.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That feels a bit contrived, I would prefer some sort of behavior observation or other method by which you could characterize the entity through the description. Also, if this is a normal wolf, how exactly does it consume rocks? Swallowing them whole, pounding them into dust, biting out of it like an apple? Again, the article feels like there are one or twelve too many corners cut outside of the interview. Why would the Foundation get to the conclusion of feeding it moon rocks, then continue feeding it moon rocks if they knew it didn't sustenance to survive?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also, there's no real established reason as to why they were feeding him in the first place so that makes me think it was just there to have as a plot point in the interview. How can it have a preference for moon rocks if it doesn't eat anything else? Why did they decide giving a wolf rocks to eat would be a good idea?

I do have a sequence for how that worked out. The wolf did not attack personnel who are not exposed to moon soil, but attacked those exposed to it. From there, they tried moon soil and realised it ate the moon soil.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoyed the interview but the rest of the article felt very lacking. If you're going for a mythological figure then surely you can do better than two paragraphs of basic description. I mean this entity has apparently been on the moon for thousands of years and there's no mention of previous sightings, evidence of itself left behind on the surface or anything at all.

Phrases like how it ceased hostility without first establishing that there was hostility and the line about its speech make the article feel slapdash. Sometimes it feels like there's an insinuation of it being outside of containment but that's not built upon and makes the whole piece feel unclear. Also, there's no real established reason as to why they were feeding him in the first place so that makes me think it was just there to have as a plot point in the interview. How can it have a preference for moon rocks if it doesn't eat anything else? Why did they decide giving a wolf rocks to eat would be a good idea?

You might have been trying to go for short n sweet but it just comes across as inconsistent and stunted. I like the interview but the rest is not ready for prime time. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is what the Foundation is created for, to answer these important questions

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. He can't beat Moon Wizard.
2. Suddenly Wolf Gut Snakes at the White House.

Maybe?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We really should just let the talking wolf eat the entire moon and see what happens

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Space SOLAR SYSTEM WOOOOLLFF

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know, I was just thinking we needed more Norse mythology. Well done. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
B. Super upset because I was drafting a very similar article. Dammit.

Reminds me of Newton and Leibniz. I suppose good ideas will have many people wanting to work on it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A. I like this
B. Super upset because I was drafting a very similar article. Dammit.

item.title: Thanks, Attribution, and Other Matters
item.content:encoded:
Allow me to express thanks to SpectralDragon, SunnyClockwork, Waterfire, Shio, mlister, Bluemopho, and Shaggydredlocks for looking through the draft.

This article features the following:

  • Lunar Area-32 by Jack Ike
  • SCP-2686 (Moon Wizard) by Wogglebug
  • LTE-2712-Bosch by Shaggydredlocks
+ show block
– hide block

Reality was warped from a previous version (that has Norse mythology as real) to the current one, where it is only a myth. The moon god has his essence become part of the physical moon. A wolf becomes scaled down to something more appropriate in the current reality.

The ten beams of light were the source of the reality shift, based on the Swords of Xuanyuan in SCP-2481 – a weapon that reduced the Xia Empire into semi-mythical status. Perhaps it affected more than just Xia? Perhaps it was not the first time the weapon was used?
